entries.name: Add Function
entries.description: Computes the sum of the inputs.

terminals.name: x+y
terminals.description: Functions © National Instruments 515 x+y is the sum of x and y. Matrix Details When you wire matrix data as an input to this function, a VI that includes subVIs that work with the matrix data type replaces the function. The resulting VI has the same icon but contains a matrix-specific algorithm. The node remains a VI if you disconnect the matrix from the inputs. Wire other data types as inputs to restore the original function. If you wire a data type to a function and that data type causes a bas

entries.name: Subtract Function
entries.description: Computes the difference of the inputs. If you wire two waveform values or two dynamic data type values to this function, error in and error out terminals appear on the function. Subtracting two time stamp values yields a numeric value (difference in time), and subtracting a numeric value from a time stamp value yields a time stamp. You cannot subtract a time stamp value from a numeric value. The dimensions of two matrices that you want to subtract must be the same. Otherwise, this function retur

terminals.name: x-y
terminals.description: x-y is the difference between x and y. Note You can manually configure this function to output data of a type you want. To specify the output data type, right-click the function and select Properties to display the Object Properties dialog box. On the Output Configuration page, click the Representation icon and select the data type you want. A blue coercion dot appears Functions

entries.name: Multiply Function
entries.description: Returns the product of the inputs. If you wire two waveform values or two dynamic data type values to this function, error in and error out terminals appear on the function. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function. on the output terminal of the function to indicate that you have configured the output data type. Functions 518 ni.com

terminals.name: x*y
terminals.description: x*y is the product of x multiplied by y. You cannot use this function to multiply a matrix and a vector. Use the A x B VI instead. When multiplying two matrices, if the number of rows in the second matrix does not match the number of columns in the first matrix, this function returns an empty matrix. Refer to the A x B VI for more information about matrix multiplication. When you wire matrix data as an input to this function, a VI that includes subVIs that work with the matrix data type replaces

entries.name: Divide Function
entries.description: Computes the quotient of the inputs.

terminals.name: x/y
terminals.description: x/y is a double-precision, floating-point number if both x and y are integers. In general, the output type is the widest representation of the inputs if the inputs are not integers or if their representations differ. Fixed-Point Details If you wire fixed-point values to this function, by default LabVIEW configures the integer word length of the quotient to avoid overflow for nonzero values of y. However, because the precision of the quotient can be infinite, rounding conditions always occur. Use
terminals.default_value: LabVIEW configures the integer word length of the quotient to avoid overflow for nonzero values of y

entries.name: Quotient & Remainder Function
entries.description: Computes the integer quotient and the remainder of the inputs. This function rounds floor(x/y) to the nearest integer towards -inf. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.name: x-y*floor(x/y)
terminals.description: x-y*floor(x/y) is the remainder. This corresponds to the modulo function of text-based programming languages. When y is 1, the remainder is the fractional part of x.

terminals.name: floor(x/y)
terminals.description: floor(x/y) is the integer quotient. If either input is a floating-point number, the quotient is a floating-point number with an integer value. When y is 1, the quotient is the integer part of x. If the integer input value of y is zero, the quotient is zero and remainder is dividend x. For floating-point inputs, if y is zero, the quotient is infinity and the remainder defaults to NaN. Functions

entries.name: To Extended Precision Float Function
entries.description: Converts a number to an extended-precision, floating-point number. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function. Functions © National Instruments 525

terminals.name: number
terminals.description: number can be a scalar number, array or cluster of numbers, array of clusters of numbers, and so on.

terminals.name: extended precision float
terminals.description: extended precision float is of the same data type structure as number.

entries.name: To Double Precision Float Function
entries.description: Converts a number to a double-precision, floating-point number. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.name: number
terminals.description: number can be a scalar number, array or cluster of numbers, array of clusters of numbers, a time stamp, and so on.

terminals.name: double precision float
terminals.description: double precision float is of the same data type structure as number.

entries.name: To Single Precision Float Function
entries.description: Converts a number to a single-precision, floating-point number. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function. Functions 526 ni.com

terminals.name: number
terminals.description: number can be a scalar number, array or cluster of numbers, array of clusters of numbers, and so on.

terminals.name: single precision float
terminals.description: single precision float is of the same data type structure as number.

entries.name: To Fixed-Point Function
entries.description: Converts any non-complex number to fixed-point representation. If you do not wire a value to the fixed-point type input or configure the output settings of this function, the data type of the fixed-point output adapts to the data you wire to the number input. For example, if you wire an 8-bit unsigned integer to the number input, LabVIEW returns an 8-bit unsigned fixed-point number with 8 integer bits. This function saturates the number by default if overflow occurs.

terminals.name: fixed-point type
terminals.description: fixed-point type is the fixed-point data type to which you want to convert the integer data. You can configure the fixed-point data type in the Data Type page of the Properties dialog box. LabVIEW ignores any data in the constant or control that you wire to fixed-point type. You also can leave this input unwired and configure the output data type in the Output Configuration page of the Properties dialog box.

terminals.name: number
terminals.description: Functions © National Instruments 527 number can be any non-complex number.

terminals.name: fixed-point
terminals.description: fixed-point is the input data scaled to the requested fixed-point data type. To override the default behavior of this function, right-click the function and select Properties from the shortcut menu to display the Output Configuration page of the Properties dialog box. You can use this dialog box to configure the output settings of this function.
terminals.default_value: behavior of this function, right-click the function and select Properties from the shortcut menu to display the Output Configuration page of the Properties dialog box

entries.name: To Quad Integer Function
entries.description: Converts a number to a 64-bit integer in the range -(2^63) to (2^63)-1. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.name: number
terminals.description: number can be a scalar number, array or cluster of numbers, array of clusters of numbers, a time stamp, and so on.

terminals.name: 64bit integer
terminals.description: 64bit integer is of the same data type structure as number. This function rounds all floating-point and fixed-point numeric values to the nearest integer. If the fractional part of the value is .5, the function rounds the value to the nearest even integer. For example, the function rounds 13.5 to 14 and rounds 14.5 to 14. Functions 528 ni.com

entries.name: To Long Integer Function
entries.description: Converts a number to a 32-bit integer in the range -(2^ 31) to (2^ 31)-1. This function rounds all floating-point and fixed-point numeric values to the nearest integer. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.name: number
terminals.description: number can be a scalar number, array or cluster of numbers, array of clusters of numbers, and so on.

terminals.name: 32bit integer
terminals.description: 32bit integer is of the same data type structure as number. If the fractional part of the floating-point or fixed-point value is .5, the function rounds the value to the nearest even integer. For example, the function rounds 13.5 to 14 and rounds 14.5 to 14.

entries.name: To Word Integer Function
entries.description: Converts a number to a 16-bit integer in the range -32,768 to 32,767. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.name: number
terminals.description: Functions © National Instruments 529 number can be a scalar number, array or cluster of numbers, array of clusters of numbers, and so on.

terminals.name: 16bit integer
terminals.description: 16bit integer is of the same data type structure as number. This function rounds all floating-point and fixed-point numeric values to the nearest integer. If the fractional part of the value is .5, the function rounds the value to the nearest even integer. For example, the function rounds 13.5 to 14 and rounds 14.5 to 14.

entries.name: To Byte Integer Function
entries.description: Converts a number to an 8-bit integer in the range -128 to 127. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.name: number
terminals.description: number can be a scalar number, array or cluster of numbers, array of clusters of numbers, and so on.

terminals.description: 8bit integer is of the same data type structure as number. This function rounds all floating-point numeric values to the nearest integer. If the fractional part of the floating-point value is .5, the function rounds the value to the nearest even integer. For example, the function rounds 13.5 to 14 and rounds 14.5 to 14. Functions 530 ni.com

entries.name: To Unsigned Quad Integer Function
entries.description: Converts a number to a 64-bit unsigned integer in the range 0 to (2^64)-1. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.description: number can be a scalar number, array or cluster of numbers, array of clusters of numbers, a time stamp, and so on.

entries.name: To Unsigned Word Integer Function
entries.description: Converts a number to a 16-bit unsigned integer in the range 0 to 65,535. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.name: number
terminals.description: number can be a scalar number, array or cluster of numbers, array of clusters of numbers, and so on.

terminals.name: unsigned 16bit integer

entries.description: Converts a number to an extended-precision, complex number. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.description: Functions © National Instruments 533 number can be a scalar number, array or cluster of numbers, array of clusters of numbers, and so on.

terminals.name: extended precision complex
terminals.description: extended precision complex is of the same data type structure as number.

entries.name: To Double Precision Complex Function
entries.description: Converts a number to a double-precision, complex number. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.description: number can be a scalar number, array or cluster of numbers, array of clusters of numbers, and so on.

entries.description: Converts a number to a single-precision, complex number. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function. Functions 534 ni.com

terminals.name: number
terminals.description: number can be a scalar number, array or cluster of numbers, array of clusters of numbers, and so on.

entries.name: Coerce To Type Function
entries.description: Converts the input data to a compatible data type while preserving the data value. Unlike the Type Cast function, this function does not reinterpret the input data. Use this function in the following cases: • To eliminate a coercion dot • To convert data without a type definition to a compatible type definition or vice versa • To rename data on the wire, such as a user event refnum

terminals.name: type
terminals.description: type specifies the data type to which you want to convert the input data x. To set the data type to which you want to convert the input data, wire a constant or control of the desired data type to type. LabVIEW ignores any data in the constant or control wired to type.

terminals.name: coerced x
terminals.description: coerced x returns the value of the input data x after the conversion. The Type Cast function also converts data to a desired data type. Unlike the Type Cast function, the Coerce To Type function allows only conversion between compatible data types to preserve the value of the input data, similar to what a coercion dot does. The Type Cast function allows conversion between incompatible data types by flattening and unflattening the input data, which allows for radical reinterpretation of the data.

entries.name: Number To Boolean Array Function
entries.description: Converts an integer or fixed-point number to a Boolean array. If you wire an integer to number, Boolean array returns an array of 8, 16, 32, or 64 elements, depending on the number of bits in the integer. If you wire a fixed-point number to number, the size of the array that Boolean array returns equals the word length of the fixed-point number. The 0th element of the array corresponds to the least significant bit of the two's complement representation of the integer.

terminals.name: number
terminals.description: number can be a number.

terminals.name: Boolean array
terminals.description: Boolean array can have 8, 16, 32, or 64 elements if number is an integer. If number is a fixed- Functions 536 ni.com point number, Boolean array can have between 1 and 64 elements. If you wire a fixed-point number that includes an overflow status to number, this function ignores the overflow status and converts only the number to a Boolean array.

entries.name: Boolean Array To Number Function
entries.description: Converts a Boolean array to an integer or a fixed-point number by interpreting the array as the binary representation of the number. The first element of the array corresponds to the least significant bit in the number.

terminals.name: Boolean array
terminals.description: Boolean array is a one-dimensional array of Boolean values. This function truncates Boolean array if it is too long. If Boolean array is too short, this function pads Boolean array according to the Sign Extension Mode you can select by right-clicking the function.

terminals.name: Never
terminals.description: This function pads Boolean array with Boolean FALSE bits.

terminals.name: If Output Is Signed
terminals.description: (default) For an output with an unsigned representation, this function pads Boolean array with Boolean FALSE bits. For an output with a signed representation, this function pads Boolean array with the last element of the array.

terminals.name: Always
terminals.description: This function pads Boolean array with the last element of the array.

terminals.name: number
terminals.description: number is an integer or a fixed-point number. You can change the numeric representation of number on the Output Configuration page of the Properties dialog box. Right-click the function and select Properties to display the Properties dialog box. The following table shows the relationship between Boolean array and number. Functions

entries.name: Boolean To (0,1) Function
entries.description: Converts a Boolean FALSE or TRUE value to a 16-bit integer with a value of 0 or 1, respectively.

terminals.name: Boolean
terminals.description: Boolean can be a scalar, an array, a cluster of Boolean values, an array of clusters of Boolean values, and so on. If Boolean is an error cluster, only the status parameter of the error cluster passes to the input terminal.
0, 1 is 0 if Boolean is FALSE and 1 if Boolean is TRUE.

entries.name: To Time Stamp Function
entries.description: Converts a number to a timestamp. Functions 538 ni.com

terminals.name: number
terminals.description: number can be a scalar number, array or cluster of numbers, array of clusters of numbers, and so on, that represents the amount of seconds from the epoch time of 12 a.m., January 1, 1904 [01-01-1904 00:00:00].

terminals.name: time stamp
terminals.description: time stamp is a time-zone-independent number of seconds that have elapsed since 12:00 a.m., Friday, January 1, 1904, Universal Time [01-01-1904 00:00:00].

entries.name: String To Byte Array Function
entries.description: Converts a string into an array of unsigned bytes. Each byte in the array has the ASCII value of the corresponding character in the string.

terminals.name: string
terminals.description: string is the input string the function converts.

terminals.name: unsigned byte array
terminals.description: unsigned byte array is the output array. The first byte in the array has the ASCII value of the first character in string, the second byte has the second value, and so on.

entries.name: Byte Array To String Function
entries.description: Converts an array of unsigned bytes representing ASCII characters into a string. Functions © National Instruments 539

terminals.name: unsigned byte array
terminals.description: unsigned byte array is the array of ASCII values you want to convert.

terminals.name: string
terminals.description: string is the result of interpreting each array element as an ASCII value and forming a string out of the corresponding characters. Refer to ASCII Codes for the numbers that correspond to each character.

entries.name: Convert Unit
entries.description: Converts a physical number (a number that has a unit) to a pure number (a number with no units) or a pure number to a physical number. Right-click the function and select Build Unit String from the shortcut menu to build and edit a string of units.

entries.name: Cast Unit Bases Function
entries.description: Changes the base units associated with the input to the base units associated with unit and returns the results at the output terminal. Functions © National Instruments 541

terminals.name: unit (none)
terminals.description: unit associates input with output results. The default is none, which does not associate any units.
terminals.default_value: none, which does not associate any units

entries.name: Color to RGB
entries.description: Resolves any color input, including system colors, into its respective red, green, and blue components. To perform a task such as color arithmetic with symbolic colors, you must convert the colors to their red, green, and blue components. Caution Use this function with extreme caution. Because this function works with bases, you must understand the conversion from an arbitrary unit to its bases before you can effectively use this function. This function can change base units, such as changing me

terminals.name: Color
terminals.description: Color is the color that you want to convert to its respective red, green, and blue components.

terminals.name: Resolved Color
terminals.description: resolved color is the resolved color in RGB format.

entries.name: Enum to Array of Enums
entries.description: Creates an array of enums with the same number of elements as values in the enum.

terminals.name: enum
terminals.description: enum specifies the input enum.

terminals.name: array of enums
terminals.description: array of enums returns an array containing each enum value in order. For example, if enum has values {cat, dog, bird}, array of enums returns a three-element array with values cat, dog, and bird, respectively.

entries.name: Increment Function
entries.description: Adds 1 to the input value. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function. Functions 544 ni.com

terminals.name: x+1
terminals.description: x+1 is the result of x+1. If the input is a time stamp value, this function increments the time by one second. If the input is an enumerated type value, this function increments the last enumerated value to the first. Examples Refer to the following example files included with LabVIEW.

entries.name: Decrement Function
entries.description: Subtracts 1 from the input value. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function. Note You can manually configure this function to output data of a type you want. To specify the output data type, right-click the function and select Properties to display the Object Properties dialog box. On the Output Configuration page, click the Representation icon and select the data type you want. A blue coercion dot appears on the output terminal of the function to indicate

terminals.name: x-1
terminals.description: x-1 is the result of x-1. If the input is a time stamp value, this function decrements the time by one second. If the input is an enumerated type value, this function decrements the first enumerated value to the last. Examples Refer to the following example files included with LabVIEW.

entries.name: Add Array Elements Function
entries.description: Returns the sum of all the elements in numeric array. Note You can manually configure this function to output data of a type you want. To specify the output data type, right-click the function and select Properties to display the Object Properties dialog box. On the Output Configuration page, click the Representation icon and select the data type you want. A blue coercion dot appears on the output terminal of the function to indicate that you have configured the output data type. Functions 546 n

terminals.name: numeric array
terminals.description: numeric array can have any number of dimensions.

terminals.name: sum
terminals.description: sum is of the same data type and structure as the elements in numeric array. Examples Refer to the following example files included with LabVIEW.

entries.name: Multiply Array Elements Function
entries.description: Returns the product of all the elements in numeric array. If numeric array is an empty array, the function returns a value of 1. If numeric array contains only one element, the function returns that element. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function. Note You cannot use this function with fixed-point numbers. If you wire fixed-point numbers to this function, the VI appears with a broken Run button. Functions © National Instruments 547

terminals.name: numeric array
terminals.description: numeric array can have any number of dimensions.

terminals.name: product
terminals.description: product is of the same data type and structure as the elements in numeric array. Examples Refer to the following example files included with LabVIEW.

entries.name: Compound Arithmetic
entries.description: Performs arithmetic on one or more numeric, array, cluster, or Boolean inputs. To select the operation (Add, Multiply, AND, OR, or XOR), right-click the function and select Change Mode from the shortcut menu. When you select this function from the Numeric palette, the default mode is Add. When you select this function from the Boolean palette, the default mode is OR. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.name: value
terminals.description: Note You cannot use this function with fixed-point numbers. If you wire fixed-point numbers to this function, the VI appears with a broken Run button. Functions 548 ni.com value 0..n-1 can be a number or Boolean value, an array of numbers or Boolean values, a cluster, array of clusters, and so on. You can wire a waveform to only one value input. If an input is a waveform, you can have an unlimited number of scalar inputs of varying sizes. If value is an error cluster, only the status parameter o

terminals.name: result
terminals.description: result returns the result of the selected operation applied to the value 0..n-1. For AND, OR, or XOR, result returns the bitwise operations on numeric inputs and logical operations on Boolean inputs. Add inputs to the node by right-clicking an input and selecting Add Input from the shortcut menu or by resizing the function. You can invert the inputs or the output of this function by right-clicking the individual terminals and selecting Invert from the shortcut menu. For Add, select Invert to neg

entries.name: Data Manipulation
entries.description: Use the Data Manipulation functions to modify the data types used in LabVIEW. Note You cannot use this function with fixed-point numbers. If you wire fixed-point numbers to this function, the VI appears with a broken Run button. Functions © National Instruments 549 Palette Object Description Type Cast Function Casts x to the data type, type, by flattening it and unflattening it using the new data type. If the function must reinterpret data instead of transforming it, LabVIEW uses a temporary buf

terminals.name: type
terminals.description: type is the data type to which you want to convert the data. Wire any constant or control to type to set the data type to which you want to convert the data. LabVIEW ignores any data in the constant or control you wire to type.

entries.name: Type Cast Function
entries.description: Casts x to the data type, type, by flattening it and unflattening it using the new data type. If the function must reinterpret data instead of transforming it, LabVIEW uses a temporary buffer. This function has the risk of reinterpreting data. To preserve the data value while converting the data, use the Coerce To Type function instead.

terminals.name: type
terminals.description: type is the data type to which you want to convert the data. Wire any constant or control to type to set the data type to which you want to convert the data. LabVIEW ignores any data in the constant or control you wire to type.

terminals.name: *(type *) &x
terminals.description: *(type *) &x is the value provided in x and converted to the same data type as type. Choosing between the Type Cast Function and the Coerce To Type Function The Coerce To Type function also converts data to a desired data type. Unlike the Coerce To Type function, the Type Cast function allows conversion between incompatible data types by flattening and unflattening the input data, which allows for radical reinterpretation of the data. When the desired data type requires a different memory size t

entries.name: Flatten To String Function
entries.description: Converts the anything input to a flattened data string of binary values. You also can use this function to convert the byte order, or endian format, of the data in the flattened data string.

terminals.name: anything
terminals.description: Functions © National Instruments 553 anything contains the data you want to convert to a UTF-8 JSON string. This input accepts arrays and clusters of Booleans, floating-point numbers, and strings. anything can also accept an array of clusters, or a cluster of arrays, of these data types. This input does not support other data types, such as enums, refnums, file paths, and fixed-point numbers. Cluster elements may be named or unnamed, but not a combination of both. If cluster elements are named,

terminals.name: prepend array or string size? (T)
terminals.description: prepend array or string size? specifies whether LabVIEW includes data size information at the beginning of data string when anything is an array or string. If prepend array or string size? is FALSE, LabVIEW does not include the size information. The default is TRUE. The string created by the Flatten To String function is a LabVIEW string. If prepend array or string size? is TRUE, the LabVIEW string has a 4-byte (I32) number at the beginning of the string that tells how long the string is. This e

terminals.name: byte order (0:big-endian, network order)
terminals.description: byte order sets the endian format of the data in the resulting flattened string. Byte order, or endian form, indicates whether integers are represented in memory from most significant byte to least significant byte or vice versa. 0 big-endian, network order (default)—The most significant byte occupies the lowest memory address. 1 native, host order—Uses the byte-ordering format of the host computer. 2 little-endian—The least significant byte occupies the lowest memory address.

terminals.name: error in (no error)
terminals.description: error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality.

terminals.name: data string
terminals.description: Functions 554 ni.com data string is the flattened data generated by the function. data string might contain header information before each non-scalar component describing its size. Such a string can be stored in a file or sent over a network. If you send the string over a network, the receiver must be able to interpret it. Usually, LabVIEW stores data as noncontiguous, indirectly referenced pieces. This function copies the data in LabVIEW form into a contiguous buffer data string. Use the Unflat

terminals.name: type string (7.x only)
terminals.description: type string (7.x only) is visible only if the terminal is already wired from a previous version of LabVIEW or if you right-click the function and select Convert 7.x Data from the shortcut menu. type string (7.x only) is an encoded binary description of data string. type string (7.x only) is not the same as the type input in the Unflatten From String function. If type string (7.x only) cannot represent the data type wired to anything, this function returns an error.

terminals.name: error out
terminals.description: error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. Use the Type Cast function for conversion of data types that does not result in flattened data. If you need to flatten type descriptor data to a string that is readable by LabVIEW 7.x or earlier, right-click this function and select Convert 7.x Data from the shortcut menu. In Convert 7.x Data mode, this function shows the type string (7.x only) output and displays the icon for this function with a red 7.

entries.name: Unflatten From String Function
entries.description: Converts binary string to the type wired to type. binary string should contain flattened data of the type wired to type. You also can use this function to specify the byte order, or endian format, of the data in the binary string.

terminals.name: type
terminals.description: type is a normally constructed LabVIEW type. type is not the type string output in the Flatten To String function.

terminals.name: binary string
terminals.description: binary string is a flattened string that is typically generated by the Flatten To String function. Functions 556 ni.com If data includes array or string size? is TRUE, binary string contains header information describing its size.

terminals.name: data includes array or string size? (T)
terminals.description: data includes array or string size? indicates whether LabVIEW reads data size information from the beginning of an incoming array or string. If data includes array or string size? is TRUE, LabVIEW reads the size information from the beginning of binary string. If data includes array or string size? is FALSE, LabVIEW detects the size information from the memory size of binary string. The default is TRUE. data includes array or string size? only accesses the top-level data size information. Arrays

terminals.name: byte order (0:big-endian, network order)
terminals.description: byte order indicates the endian format of the data in the incoming flattened string. Byte order, or endian form, indicates whether integers are represented in memory from most-significant byte to least-significant byte or vice versa. 0 big-endian, network order (default)—The most-significant byte occupies the lowest memory address. 1 native, host order—Uses the byte-ordering format of the host computer. 2 little-endian—The least-significant byte occupies the lowest memory address.

terminals.name: error in (no error)
terminals.description: error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality.

terminals.name: rest of the binary string
terminals.description: rest of the binary string contains any leftover bytes that this function did not convert. This function does not convert all the bytes if the size of binary string is not a multiple of the size of type.

terminals.name: value
terminals.description: value returns binary string as unflattened data of the same data type and structure as type.

terminals.name: error out
terminals.description: Functions

entries.name: Mantissa & Exponent Function
entries.description: Returns the mantissa and exponent of the input numeric value such that number = mantissa * 2^exponent. If number is 0, both mantissa and exponent are 0. Otherwise, the absolute value of mantissa is greater than or equal to 1 and less than 2, and the value of exponent is an integer. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function. Functions 558 ni.com

terminals.name: number
terminals.description: number can be any decimal representation.

terminals.name: mantissa
terminals.description: mantissa has the same decimal representation as number.

terminals.name: exponent
terminals.description: exponent has the same decimal representation as number.

entries.name: Rotate Left With Carry Function
entries.description: Rotates each bit in the input value one bit to the left (from least significant to most significant bit), inserts carry in the low-order bit, and returns the most significant bit. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.name: carry
terminals.description: carry is the new low-order bit of value.

terminals.name: value
terminals.description: value must be an integer. It cannot be an array or a cluster.

terminals.name: msb carry out
terminals.description: msb carry out is the former high-order bit of value.

terminals.name: value
terminals.description: Functions © National Instruments 559 value is the new value. The data type of the output value is determined by the data type of the input value.

entries.name: Rotate Right With Carry Function
entries.description: Rotates each bit in value one bit to the right (from most significant to least significant), inserts carry in the high-order bit, and returns the least significant bit. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.name: carry
terminals.description: carry is the new high-order bit of value.

terminals.name: value
terminals.description: value must be an integer. It cannot be an array or a cluster.

terminals.name: lsb carry out
terminals.description: lsb carry out is the former low-order bit of value.

terminals.name: value
terminals.description: value is the new value. The data type of the output value is determined by the data type of the input value.

entries.name: Logical Shift Function
entries.description: Shifts x the number of bits specified by y. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function. Functions 560 ni.com

terminals.name: x << y
terminals.description: x << y is the result of the shift and has the same numeric representation as x.
